sections.heading: Genomic Events
sections.content: The predominant molecular pathway for vaginal squamous intraepithelial lesions (SIL) and squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is infection with high-risk human papillomavirus (HPV), with HPV16 being the most common type detected in 46% to 77% of vaginal cancers [5]. In HPV-associated tumors, viral oncoproteins E6 and E7 inactivate the tumor suppressors p53 and pRb, respectively, driving carcinogenesis. For HPV-independent SCC, which is more common in postmenopausal individuals, TP53 mutations are a key early event, leading to aberrant p53 protein accumulation detectable by immunohistochemistry (IHC) [VAG-A 1].

sections.heading: Driver Mutations
sections.content: Key driver mutations include TP53 inactivation in HPV-independent tumors. Comprehensive molecular profiling may reveal actionable alterations including NTRK gene fusions (found in ~1% of all solid tumors) [MS-7], RET gene fusions, HER2 overexpression/amplification (observed in approximately 2% to 6% of cervical cancers, a related site) [MS-8], and high tumor mutational burden (TMB) or microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) status [MS-6].

sections.heading: Signaling Pathways
sections.content: The HPV-driven pathway involves dysregulation of the p53 and pRb tumor suppressor pathways. Immune checkpoint pathways involving PD-1/PD-L1 are relevant for immunotherapy; PD-L1 expression is a predictive biomarker [MS-5].

sections.heading: Chromosomal Abnormalities
sections.content: NTRK and RET gene fusions are actionable chromosomal rearrangements recommended for testing via comprehensive genomic profiling [VAG-A 2].

sections.heading: Biomarker Prevalence
sections.content: HPV is detected in approximately 40% to 70% of all vulvar and vaginal cancers [5]. p53 aberrant expression is a hallmark of HPV-independent tumors. Comprehensive molecular profiling is recommended for advanced or metastatic disease to assess for MSI-H, TMB, NTRK, and RET fusions for pan-tumor targeted therapy opportunities [VAG-A 2].
